walkthrough.title: Choose Your Storyteller
walkthrough.text: Let's get Blood on the Clocktower set up! First, you need a Storyteller. This person does not play as a regular character. Instead, they run the game, wake players at night, provide information, and keep the story moving. The Storyteller should be someone who has played before or has read the rules thoroughly.

walkthrough.title: Pick a Script
walkthrough.text: Now let's choose which script to play. A script is a predetermined list of characters that can appear in the game. For your first game, use Trouble Brewing, which is the beginner-friendly script. Bad Moon Rising and Sects and Violets are more complex and best saved for after your group has a few games under their belt.

walkthrough.title: Count Your Players
walkthrough.text: Count how many players you have, not including the Storyteller. Blood on the Clocktower supports five to twenty players. The game scales beautifully, but we recommend around ten players for your first game. The player count determines exactly how many Townsfolk, Outsiders, Minions, and Demons are in play.

walkthrough.title: Determine Team Composition
walkthrough.text: Let's check the team breakdown for your player count. With ten players, you will have seven Townsfolk, zero Outsiders, two Minions, and one Demon. Smaller games have fewer Minions, and larger games add more of every type. The Storyteller uses the setup chart on the script sheet to determine the exact numbers.

walkthrough.title: Select Characters from the Script
walkthrough.text: The Storyteller secretly selects which specific characters from the chosen script will be in the game. They must pick exactly the right number of each type for the player count. Not every character on the script has to be used, which means players cannot be certain which characters are actually in play.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Character Tokens
walkthrough.text: The Storyteller places the selected character tokens into the bag. Each token shows the character name and icon. Make sure you have exactly one token per player. The Storyteller also sets aside any reminder tokens they will need to track abilities and status effects on the Grimoire during the game.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Character Tokens
walkthrough.text: Let's hand out roles. Each player draws one character token from the bag and looks at it secretly. Do not show your token to anyone else. Your token tells you your character name, your team alignment, and your character type. Keep it hidden for the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Grimoire
walkthrough.text: The Storyteller sets up the Grimoire, which is a large book that tracks every player's character and status throughout the game. The Storyteller places each player's character token in the Grimoire along with any reminder tokens for abilities. This is the Storyteller's private reference and no player should ever see it.

walkthrough.title: Arrange the Seating
walkthrough.text: Have all players sit in a circle or around a table. Seating matters because some abilities reference neighbors, meaning the players sitting immediately to your left and right. Once everyone is seated, the seating order is locked for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Vote Tokens
walkthrough.text: Give each player a vote token. This token has a live side and a dead side. Everyone starts on the live side. When a player dies, they flip their token to the dead side to show they have one final vote remaining. The Storyteller also keeps a life and death tracker in the Grimoire.

walkthrough.title: Review the Script Publicly
walkthrough.text: Before starting, make sure every player can see the script sheet. This sheet lists all characters that could possibly be in the game, along with a brief description of each ability. Players need this reference throughout the game to evaluate claims and bluffs. Place it centrally or hand out copies.

walkthrough.title: Run the First Night
walkthrough.text: Now let's begin the first night. The Storyteller announces that night has fallen and asks everyone to close their eyes. The Storyteller then wakes players one at a time according to the night order on the script. On the first night, Evil players learn their teammates, the Demon receives three bluff characters, and information-gathering Townsfolk receive their initial clues. When all abilities are resolved, the Storyteller announces that dawn has arrived.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how Blood on the Clocktower works. The game is a battle between Good and Evil played over a series of day and night cycles. Good wins by executing the Demon during the day. Evil wins when only two players remain alive. Everything revolves around gathering information, making accusations, and figuring out who the Demon is before it is too late.

walkthrough.title: The Four Character Types
walkthrough.text: Let's go over the four character types. Townsfolk are on the Good team and have helpful abilities like detecting Evil or gaining information. Outsiders are also on the Good team but their abilities slightly hinder the Good side. Minions are on the Evil team and work to disrupt Good or protect the Demon. The Demon is on the Evil team, kills a player each night, and is the primary target Good needs to execute.

walkthrough.title: The Day Phase: Free Discussion
walkthrough.text: Each day begins with open discussion. Everyone can talk to anyone, in public or in private whispered conversations. Players share information, make claims about their characters, accuse others of being Evil, and try to piece together the truth. There is no turn order for discussion. This is the heart of the game and you should take your time with it.

walkthrough.title: Nominations
walkthrough.text: When the town is ready, any living player may nominate another player for execution. You can nominate anyone, alive or dead, though dead players cannot nominate. Only one nomination can be active at a time. Once someone is nominated, the nominee gets a chance to defend themselves with a brief speech before the vote.

walkthrough.title: Voting and Execution
walkthrough.text: After the nominee speaks, the Storyteller calls for a vote going around the circle. Each living player raises their hand or stays silent. Dead players may also vote, but they only get one vote for the entire game once they use it. A nomination succeeds if the number of votes meets or exceeds half the number of living players. The player with the most votes at the end of the day is executed.

walkthrough.title: Only One Execution Per Day
walkthrough.text: Let's clarify an important rule. Only one player can be executed per day, even if multiple nominations occur. If a second nomination gets more votes than the first, the new nominee becomes the execution candidate instead. The town can also choose to execute nobody if no nomination reaches the required threshold. Choosing not to execute is a valid strategic decision.

walkthrough.title: The Night Phase
walkthrough.text: When the day ends, night falls. The Storyteller asks everyone to close their eyes. Then the Storyteller wakes characters one at a time in a specific order to use their abilities. The Demon chooses a player to kill. Information-gathering characters receive their clues. Some characters can protect others or take special actions. When all abilities are resolved, the Storyteller announces dawn and reveals who died in the night.

walkthrough.title: Death and the Ghost Vote
walkthrough.text: When a player dies, whether by execution or by the Demon's night kill, they are out of the game in terms of abilities. However, dead players stay involved. They can still participate in all discussions and conversations. They lose their character ability but retain one final vote they can use on any future nomination. Dead players cannot nominate.

walkthrough.title: How Good Wins
walkthrough.text: Good wins the game by executing the Demon during the day. It does not matter how many Good players have died along the way. If the town correctly identifies and executes the Demon, Good wins immediately. This is the only way for Good to win, so all of your information gathering and deduction should be aimed at finding the Demon.

walkthrough.title: How Evil Wins
walkthrough.text: Evil wins when only two players remain alive. The Demon kills one player each night, so the town is always shrinking. If the town executes too many Good players or fails to find the Demon quickly enough, Evil will eventually whittle the living players down to two. At that point, Evil wins regardless of who those two players are.

walkthrough.title: The Demon's Bluffs
walkthrough.text: At the start of the game, the Storyteller secretly gives the Demon three bluff characters. These are Good-aligned characters from the script that are not actually in play. The Demon can safely claim to be one of these characters because no other player has that role. The Demon shares these bluffs with their Minions so the Evil team can coordinate their cover stories.

walkthrough.title: Poisoned and Drunk
walkthrough.text: Some abilities cause players to become poisoned or drunk. A poisoned or drunk player's ability malfunctions. They might receive false information from the Storyteller, or their protective ability might simply not work. The critical rule is that the affected player is never told they are poisoned or drunk. They believe their information is real, which creates wonderful uncertainty.

walkthrough.title: The Storyteller's Role
walkthrough.text: The Storyteller is more than a neutral referee. They have discretion over how certain abilities resolve and can adjust outcomes to keep the game balanced and fun. If a game is too easy for one side, the Storyteller can nudge information in ways that create a more interesting experience. The Storyteller's goal is an entertaining game, not a perfectly mechanical one.

walkthrough.title: Private Conversations
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about one of the most unique features of the game. Players can have private whispered conversations at any time during the day, not just during formal discussion periods. You can pull someone aside, whisper in their ear, or pass notes. This creates a web of private information exchange that makes every game feel dynamic and social.

walkthrough.title: Lying is Encouraged
walkthrough.text: There are no rules against lying in Blood on the Clocktower. Evil players will lie about their character, their information, and their suspicions. But Good players can also bluff to protect themselves or draw out Evil. A Townsfolk might claim to be a different character to avoid being targeted by the Demon. Deception is a core part of the experience for both teams.

walkthrough.title: How a Full Day-Night Cycle Works
walkthrough.text: Let's recap the full cycle. Dawn breaks and the Storyteller announces who died in the night. The town enters free discussion, talking in public and private. Players nominate suspects and vote on executions. At most one player is executed. Night falls, everyone closes their eyes, characters use abilities, and the Demon kills. Then a new day begins. This cycle repeats until Good executes the Demon or only two players remain alive.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Trouble Brewing
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Blood on the Clocktower! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted game with ten players using the Trouble Brewing script. We will follow the action across multiple day and night cycles as the town tries to find the Demon. Along the way, players will make some common beginner mistakes so we can learn from them.

walkthrough.title: The Setup
walkthrough.text: Our ten players draw their character tokens from the bag. The Storyteller has selected the following characters: Washerwoman, Librarian, Investigator, Chef, Empath, Fortune Teller, and Undertaker as the seven Townsfolk. The two Minions are the Poisoner and the Scarlet Woman. The Demon is the Imp. Everyone looks at their token secretly.

walkthrough.title: The First Night
walkthrough.text: Night falls. The Storyteller wakes the Poisoner first, who chooses to poison the Empath. Then the Minions and the Imp open their eyes together to learn each other's identities. The Imp receives three bluff characters: Monk, Ravenkeeper, and Mayor. Now the information Townsfolk are woken one by one to receive their clues. The Empath gets information, but because they are poisoned, the Storyteller may give them false data.

walkthrough.title: Day One: Discussion Begins
walkthrough.text: Dawn arrives and nobody died since it was the first night. The town launches into discussion. The Washerwoman shares that they know one of two players is the Librarian. The Chef announces they detected one pair of Evil neighbors. The Empath, unknowingly poisoned, confidently declares that neither of their neighbors is Evil, but this information is actually wrong.

walkthrough.title: Mistake One: Trusting Information Absolutely
walkthrough.text: Player seven, who is the Empath, is completely confident in their information. They loudly declare that their neighbors are definitely Good and the town should trust them without question. This is a common beginner mistake. Because poisoning and drunkenness can give false information without the player knowing, no single piece of information should ever be treated as absolute truth.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Consider Poisoning
walkthrough.text: The key lesson here is that every piece of information in Blood on the Clocktower could be wrong. The Poisoner can target a different player each night, corrupting their ability. Even if you are a Townsfolk with a powerful information ability, you might be poisoned and not know it. Always hold your information as probably true rather than certainly true, and cross-reference with what other players are claiming.

walkthrough.title: Day One: The First Nomination
walkthrough.text: After lots of discussion, player three nominates player nine for execution. Player nine gives a defense speech claiming to be the Fortune Teller and shares some information. The town votes, and the nomination gets enough votes to put player nine on the block. But player nine is actually the Fortune Teller, a valuable Good role.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Two: Executing Too Aggressively
walkthrough.text: The town decides to execute player nine on day one with very little evidence. This is the second common mistake. Every execution of a Good player brings the town closer to Evil's win condition of two players remaining. With ten players, Good can only afford a few wrong executions before the Demon's nightly kills and the town's own executions shrink the population to two. The town should gather more information before pulling the trigger.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Executions Cost Lives
walkthrough.text: Remember that Evil wins when only two players are alive. The Demon removes one player per night, and each daytime execution removes another. If the town executes a Good player, that is effectively two Good players lost per cycle. Be deliberate about executions. It is often better to skip an execution on an early day and gather more information than to gamble on thin evidence.

walkthrough.title: Night Two: The Demon Strikes
walkthrough.text: Night falls again. The Poisoner chooses a new target. The Imp kills player two, the Librarian. Dawn comes, and the Storyteller announces that player two died in the night. The Undertaker, who learns the character of the person executed the previous day, is told that player nine was the Fortune Teller. This confirms the town made a mistake yesterday.

walkthrough.title: Day Two: Evil Stays Quiet
walkthrough.text: Discussion begins, and the town is buzzing about the Undertaker's reveal. Meanwhile, player five, who is the Scarlet Woman Minion, has been sitting silently through both days. They have not shared any claims, have not engaged in discussions, and have not offered any theories. They are hoping to avoid attention by saying nothing.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Three: Evil Players Staying Silent
walkthrough.text: Player five's silence is actually drawing suspicion rather than deflecting it. In a game where everyone is expected to share information and participate in deduction, a player who says nothing stands out. Good players naturally discuss and theorize. An Evil player who stays quiet is not blending in; they are painting a target on themselves by behaving differently from the engaged Good players.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Evil Must Actively Participate
walkthrough.text: The lesson for Evil players is that you must actively participate in discussions. Claim a Good character, share fabricated information that sounds plausible, accuse other players, and engage in the social fabric of the game. The Demon received three bluff characters specifically so that Evil can make convincing claims. Staying silent is one of the easiest ways to get caught as Evil.

walkthrough.title: Day Two: Narrowing the Suspects
walkthrough.text: The town starts cross-referencing information. The Chef's one-pair-of-Evil-neighbors result helps narrow down which pairs of adjacent players might be Evil. The Investigator shares their findings about a potential Minion. Players begin to build a picture of who might be the Imp. The quiet player five is now being questioned aggressively.

walkthrough.title: The Imp Plays It Cool
walkthrough.text: Player eight, who is the Imp, has been playing brilliantly. They claimed to be the Monk on day one, which is one of their bluff characters, and have been actively participating in discussions and pointing fingers at other players. The Imp is demonstrating good Evil play by staying engaged and building a credible cover story.

walkthrough.title: Day Two: A Better Execution
walkthrough.text: After thorough discussion, the town nominates player five, the suspiciously quiet Scarlet Woman. The vote passes and player five is executed. The Undertaker will learn tonight that the executed player was the Scarlet Woman, confirming the town found a Minion. This is progress, but the Demon is still alive.

walkthrough.title: The Final Days
walkthrough.text: Over the next two days and nights, the town continues to lose players to the Demon's kills while debating who to execute. By cross-referencing the Washerwoman's, Investigator's, and Chef's information, and factoring in who might have been poisoned, the town finally narrows the Demon down to player eight. They nominate and execute the Imp. The Storyteller reveals it was the Demon. Good wins!

walkthrough.title: Congratulations and Key Takeaways
walkthrough.text: Congratulations on playing through your first game of Blood on the Clocktower! You saw three important lessons in action. First, never trust information absolutely because poisoning can corrupt any ability. Second, do not execute too aggressively because every wrong execution helps Evil reach two alive. Third, Evil players must actively participate and bluff to avoid standing out.

walkthrough.title: Debrief and Reveal
walkthrough.text: After the game, the Storyteller opens the Grimoire and reveals everything. You will see who was poisoned, what information was true versus false, and how the Evil team coordinated. This debrief is one of the best parts of Blood on the Clocktower because it lets everyone understand what really happened behind the scenes.

walkthrough.title: Ready for More
walkthrough.text: Before your next game, check out General Tips for deeper strategy on both the Good and Evil sides. Reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all character types, status effects, and terminology. And remember, you can always ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask a question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Cross-Reference Everything
walkthrough.text: The most powerful tool in Blood on the Clocktower is cross-referencing multiple sources of information. No single player's claim should be taken at face value because of the possibility of poisoning, drunkenness, or outright lying. When three or four players' claims all point in the same direction, that is much stronger than any one claim alone. Build a web of information and look for where the threads agree.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Execution Budget
walkthrough.text: Think of executions as a limited resource. Count the living players, subtract two for Evil's win condition, and factor in the Demon's nightly kills. That tells you roughly how many executions the town can afford before Evil wins. Early in the game, prioritize gathering information over executing on weak evidence. Save your executions for when you have strong cross-referenced leads.

walkthrough.title: Playing Good: Share Information Wisely
walkthrough.text: As a Good player, sharing your information is usually beneficial, but timing and framing matter. If you share everything on day one, Evil knows exactly which abilities to target with the Poisoner. Consider holding back some details until you can verify them against other claims. When you do share, explain your reasoning so other Good players can evaluate your logic.

walkthrough.title: Playing Evil: Build a Believable Story
walkthrough.text: As an Evil player, your survival depends on having a credible character claim and consistent information to share. Use the Demon's three bluff characters to craft a convincing cover. Fabricate information that is plausible but subtly steers the town away from the real Demon. The best Evil players contribute actively to discussions so they look like helpful Good players.

walkthrough.title: Use Private Conversations Strategically
walkthrough.text: Private whispered conversations are one of the most powerful tools in the game. Use them to share sensitive information with players you trust, to test someone's reaction to an accusation, or to coordinate with allies. Be aware that having too many private conversations can look suspicious to the group. Balance private and public communication.

walkthrough.title: Pay Attention to the Storyteller
walkthrough.text: The Storyteller's behavior can give subtle clues about the game state. How long they spend at night, which direction they walk during the wake-up phase, and their facial expressions during reveals can all be informative. Experienced players pay attention to these cues without relying on them too heavily. Remember that the Storyteller also has discretion, so not everything is purely mechanical.

walkthrough.title: Dead Players Still Matter
walkthrough.text: Do not disengage when you die. Dead players can still discuss, share information, and influence the town's decisions. Your one remaining ghost vote is incredibly valuable in a close endgame vote. Stay involved, keep analyzing, and use your unique perspective as a dead player. You know your own character was real, which is a confirmed data point the town can trust.

walkthrough.title: Embrace the Chaos
walkthrough.text: Blood on the Clocktower is designed to be messy and uncertain. Information is unreliable, players lie, and the Storyteller has discretion. Do not try to solve the game like a logic puzzle with perfect information. Instead, embrace the social deduction aspect. Read people, build trust relationships, take calculated risks, and enjoy the dramatic moments. The best games are the ones where the town barely figures it out in time.

summary.title: Cross-Reference Everything
bullets: No single claim is reliable — poisoning, drunkenness, or lies
3-4 claims pointing the same direction = strong evidence
Build a web of info and look for where threads agree

summary.title: Manage Your Execution Budget
bullets: Count living players → subtract 2 (Evil win) → factor in nightly kills
That = roughly how many executions you can afford
Early game: prioritize info over weak-evidence executions

summary.title: Playing Good: Share Information Wisely
bullets: Sharing info helps, but timing matters
Day 1 oversharing → Evil targets you with Poisoner
Verify claims against others before revealing everything

summary.title: Playing Evil: Build a Believable Story
bullets: Use Demon's 3 bluff characters for credible claims
Fabricate plausible info that steers town away from Demon
Actively contribute to discussions — look like helpful Good

summary.title: Use Private Conversations Strategically
bullets: Whisper to share sensitive info, test reactions, coordinate
Too many private conversations can look suspicious
Balance private and public communication

summary.title: Pay Attention to the Storyteller
bullets: Night phase duration, walking direction, expressions = subtle cues
Use these observations but do not rely on them solely
Storyteller has discretion — not everything is mechanical

summary.title: Dead Players Still Matter
bullets: Dead players discuss, share info, influence decisions
1 ghost vote = critical in close endgame votes
Your confirmed character is a trusted data point

summary.title: Embrace the Chaos
bullets: Info is unreliable, players lie, Storyteller has discretion
Not a logic puzzle — it is social deduction
Read people, build trust, take calculated risks

walkthrough.title: The Starpass: Imp Self-Kill
walkthrough.text: In Trouble Brewing, the Imp can kill itself at night. When this happens, a living Minion becomes the new Demon. This is called a starpass. It is a devastating Evil strategy because the town may think they found the Demon when the old Imp dies, but Evil continues with a new Demon. Watch for suspicious night deaths where the victim was already under heavy suspicion.

walkthrough.title: Soft Claims and Hard Claims
walkthrough.text: Advanced players distinguish between soft claims and hard claims. A soft claim is hinting at your role without stating it outright, like saying you received information about a pair of players. A hard claim is directly stating your character. Soft claiming early and hard claiming later lets you gather reactions before committing. Evil players can use this to gauge what roles are already claimed before choosing their bluff.

walkthrough.title: Good Bluffing as a Townsfolk
walkthrough.text: Good players can and should sometimes lie about their character. If you are an important information role like the Empath, claiming to be a less valuable character protects you from the Poisoner and the Demon's kill. Later, when you have gathered enough information, you can reveal your true role with the data you have collected. This is called sandbagging and is a legitimate advanced tactic.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Claim Space
walkthrough.text: Track every character claim made throughout the game. Since you know the script and the player count, you know exactly how many of each type should exist. If more players claim Townsfolk roles than there are Townsfolk in the game, at least one person is lying. The claim space gets tighter as the game progresses, making it harder for Evil to maintain their bluffs without conflicting with someone else's claim.

walkthrough.title: Poisoner Target Prediction
walkthrough.text: If you are on the Good team, try to predict who the Poisoner will target each night. The Poisoner typically targets the player who seems most dangerous to Evil. If you can anticipate this, you can discount that player's information for that night. Conversely, information from a player the Poisoner would have no reason to target is more likely to be accurate. This meta-level reasoning adds a layer of reliability assessment to all information.

walkthrough.title: The Final Three Dilemma
walkthrough.text: When only three players remain alive, the town faces a critical decision. Two are Good and one is the Demon. The town must execute the Demon today or Evil wins tomorrow night. At this point, all previous information needs to be distilled into a single binary choice. Experienced players plan for this moment throughout the game by building ironclad trust with at least one other player so that final decision is as informed as possible.

walkthrough.title: Evil Team Coordination
walkthrough.text: Advanced Evil play requires tight coordination between the Demon and the Minions. The Demon should share bluff characters with Minions early through private conversations. Minions should avoid claiming the same role or giving conflicting fabricated information. One common strategy is for a Minion to deliberately get caught late in the game, drawing the execution away from the Demon on a critical day.

walkthrough.title: Storyteller Psychology
walkthrough.text: In higher-level play, understanding the Storyteller's tendencies becomes important. Some Storytellers give generous information when Good is losing, while others play it straight. Some use ability discretion to create dramatic moments. Over multiple games with the same Storyteller, you can learn their style and factor it into your reads. The Storyteller is not your enemy; they want a great game, and understanding their approach helps you interpret the information you receive.

summary.title: The Starpass: Imp Self-Kill
bullets: Imp kills itself at night → a living Minion becomes the new Demon
Town may think they found the Demon, but Evil continues
Watch for suspicious night deaths of already-suspected players

summary.title: Soft Claims and Hard Claims
bullets: Soft claim = hint at role without stating it directly
Hard claim = explicitly declare your character
Soft claim early → gauge reactions → hard claim later

summary.title: Good Bluffing as a Townsfolk
bullets: Claim a less valuable role to protect yourself from Poisoner/Demon
Reveal true role later with collected data — called sandbagging
Legitimate advanced tactic for important info roles

summary.title: Reading the Claim Space
bullets: Track all character claims vs known type distribution
More claims than slots → someone is lying
Claim space tightens as game progresses → harder for Evil to bluff

summary.title: Poisoner Target Prediction
bullets: Predict who Poisoner targets → discount that player's info
Poisoner usually targets the most dangerous-looking Good player
Untargeted player info = more likely accurate

summary.title: The Final Three Dilemma
bullets: 3 alive: 2 Good + 1 Demon → must execute correctly today
Wrong execution → Evil wins tomorrow night
Build ironclad trust with 1+ player throughout the game for this moment

summary.title: Evil Team Coordination
bullets: Share bluffs early via private conversations
Avoid conflicting claims between Demon and Minions
Minion can deliberately get caught late to shield Demon

summary.title: Storyteller Psychology
bullets: Learn Storyteller tendencies over multiple games
Some give generous info when Good is losing, others play straight
Storyteller wants a great game — understanding their style helps reads

entries.definition: The team trying to find and execute the Demon. Comprises Townsfolk and Outsiders. Good wins immediately when the Demon is executed during the day.
The team trying to eliminate Good players until only two remain alive. Comprises Minions and the Demon. Evil wins when only two players are left alive, regardless of alignment.
Good-aligned characters with helpful abilities such as detecting Evil, gaining information about specific players, or protecting allies. The majority of players in any game are Townsfolk.

Good-aligned characters whose abilities slightly hinder the Good team. Examples include the Drunk, who thinks they are a Townsfolk but has a malfunctioning ability. Fewer Outsiders appear in most games.
Evil-aligned characters who support the Demon by disrupting Good or providing cover. Minions know who the Demon is after the first night. The number of Minions scales with player count.

The primary Evil character who kills one player each night. Good must execute the Demon to win. The Demon receives three bluff characters to help maintain a cover story. There is always exactly one Demon in play.
The act of a living player proposing another player for execution. Only living players can nominate. Each player can only nominate once per day, and each player can only be nominated once per day.
After being nominated, the nominee may give a brief speech to defend themselves before the vote. This is their chance to share information, deny accusations, or plead their case to the town.

After a nomination and defense speech, the Storyteller goes around the circle and each player may raise their hand to vote. Votes must meet or exceed half the number of living players to put someone on the block.
The act of killing a player by majority vote during the day. Only one execution can occur per day. The player with the most votes above the threshold is executed. The town may choose not to execute.

Players may whisper privately to each other at any time during the day. This allows sharing sensitive information or testing reactions away from the group. A key social mechanic unique to Blood on the Clocktower.

The specific sequence in which the Storyteller wakes characters during the night. Each script has a defined night order to ensure abilities resolve correctly. The Storyteller follows this order using the Grimoire.

Each night, the Demon wakes and chooses one player to kill. That player dies and is announced at dawn. The Demon cannot typically kill themselves or other Evil players, though some Demon types have exceptions.

Many Townsfolk wake at night to receive information from the Storyteller. This may include learning about specific players, detecting Evil among neighbors, or identifying character types. Information may be false if the player is poisoned or drunk.

The opening night before the first day. Evil players learn each other's identities, the Demon receives three bluff characters, and information Townsfolk get their initial data. No one dies on the first night since the Demon has not killed yet.

A status effect caused by certain characters like the Poisoner. A poisoned player's ability malfunctions and they may receive false information from the Storyteller. The poisoned player is never told they are poisoned.

A status effect similar to poisoned. A drunk player's ability does not work correctly and they may receive false information. The Drunk Outsider character permanently has this status but believes they are a different Townsfolk character.

A player who has been killed by execution or the Demon. Dead players lose their character ability but may still participate in discussions. They retain one ghost vote usable on any future nomination. Dead players cannot nominate.

The beginner script designed for learning the game. Features straightforward characters with clear abilities. Key characters include the Washerwoman, Librarian, Investigator, Chef, Empath, Fortune Teller, Undertaker, Monk, Ravenkeeper, and Mayor as Townsfolk.

The intermediate script that introduces more complex interactions and death-related abilities. Characters are more nuanced and the game features more ways to die or prevent death. Recommended after several Trouble Brewing games.

The advanced script featuring character-swapping, madness mechanics, and more intricate information puzzles. Players may change alignment or character during the game. Recommended for experienced groups only.
Blood on the Clocktower has no individual scoring or victory points. The entire Good team wins together or the entire Evil team wins together. Dead players on the winning team still win.

The facilitator who runs the game. The Storyteller selects characters, manages the night phase, provides information to players, tracks statuses in the Grimoire, and has discretion to adjust ability outcomes for game balance.

The Storyteller's private reference book that tracks every player's character, status effects, and reminder tokens. No player should ever see the Grimoire during the game. It is revealed during the post-game debrief.

A curated list of characters available for a specific game. Each script defines which Townsfolk, Outsiders, Minions, and Demons can appear. The Storyteller selects characters only from the chosen script.

Three out-of-play Good characters given secretly to the Demon at the start of the game. Since no player has these roles, the Demon and Minions can safely claim them. These are selected from the script's unused characters.

The single remaining vote a dead player can use at any point in the game. Once used, it is gone permanently. Dead players often save their ghost vote for a critical endgame nomination where it could determine the outcome.

A player who has received enough votes to be the current execution candidate. If another nomination receives more votes, the new nominee replaces them on the block. The player on the block at the end of the day is executed.

The players sitting immediately to your left and right in the seating circle. Some character abilities reference neighbors, making seating position strategically relevant. Seating is locked once the game begins.
An informal term for when the Imp kills itself at night, causing a living Minion to become the new Demon. This advanced Evil strategy can mislead the town into thinking the Demon has been found.
